one_line_takeaway: Injecting just five crafted image-text pairs into a ~480k-entry multimodal knowledge base can force VLM-RAG systems (including Claude-3.5 Sonnet and GPT-4o) to emit attacker-chosen answers ~94-98% of the time, and no single tested defense stops both the dirty-label and clean-label variants.
key_finding: With only 5 injected image-text pairs per target query, Poisoned-MRAG drives attack success to near-ceiling while collapsing utility. On InfoSeek the dirty-label attack reaches ASR-R 1.00 / ASR-G 0.98 and the clean-label reaches ASR-R 0.97 / ASR-G 0.94, versus a no-attack ACC of ~0.94 that falls to ~0.02-0.06 under attack. It dominates all five baselines by a wide margin -- the strongest baseline (CLIP PGD) reaches only ASR-R 0.19 / ASR-G 0.18 on InfoSeek and 0.63 / 0.32 on OVEN, while prompt-injection and corpus-poisoning baselines are near 0. High ASRs hold across all eight VLMs (including Claude-3.5 Sonnet: clean-label ASR-G 0.94, dirty-label 0.98) and transfer across retrievers (dirty-label ViT-H-14 ASR-R 0.98). No single evaluated defense fully counters both variants: the two attacks have complementary weaknesses, so a defender must choose which to blunt.
core_contribution: Poisoned-MRAG is presented as the first knowledge-poisoning attack designed specifically for multimodal (image+text) RAG systems that augment vision-language models (VLMs). The paper's own contribution is a cross-modal attack that injects a small number (N=5 per target query) of crafted image-text pairs into the multimodal knowledge database so that, for attacker-chosen target queries, the VLM emits an attacker-chosen target answer. It formalizes the attack as an optimization problem and decomposes it into two necessary conditions -- a retrieval condition (the malicious pair must out-rank benign pairs for the target query) and a generation condition (the malicious pair alone must induce the target answer). It contributes two attack strategies: a dirty-label attack (image and text need not be semantically aligned; reuses the query image directly, works in the restricted/no-retriever-access setting) and a clean-label attack (semantically aligned, stealthy image generated by DALL-E-3 then PGD-refined to match the query embedding, for the full-retriever-access setting). Note: the two-condition retrieval/generation decomposition is explicitly adapted from the cited single-modal PoisonedRAG (ref [63]); the paper's novelty is extending it cross-modally and the image-crafting machinery, not the two-condition idea itself.
threat_model: No-box attacker who cannot access the composition of the knowledge database, does not know the victim VLM's name or parameters, and cannot query the VLM. The attacker selects an arbitrary set of M target queries (each an image-text pair) and assigns each an arbitrary target (incorrect) answer, then injects N<<d malicious image-text pairs per query into the database (e.g., 5 pairs into a 481,782-pair corpus). Two knowledge settings distinguish the attacks: restricted-access (no access to the retriever -> dirty-label attack, a genuine no-box threat) and full-access (attacker knows the retriever's structure/parameters -> clean-label attack, i.e., white-box retriever but still black-box VLM). Stated real-world impact scenarios: brand-promotion fraud, medical misdiagnosis, and manipulated autonomous-driving signals. The paper acknowledges the dirty-label attack is detectable under strict content moderation because the injected image mismatches its text, which motivates the stealthy clean-label variant.
limitations: Clean-label attack assumes full white-box access to the retriever (structure and parameters) and its ASR degrades on unseen retrievers (ViT-H-14: ASR-R 0.80 / ASR-G 0.76 vs CLIP-SF 0.97/0.94). Dirty-label, though no-box, is image/text-mismatched and thus detectable by content moderation, and is substantially neutralized by SHA-256 duplicate removal (InfoSeek ASR-R 1.00->0.33, ASR-G 0.98->0.54) because identical injected images collapse to an effective N=1. Clean-label ASR-G drops sharply when k>N unless N is raised. Evaluation uses only 50 target queries per dataset and only two benchmarks; the attack is targeted (attacker pre-selects queries and answers) rather than a general corpus corruption. Structure-driven defenses reduce ASR but at heavy utility cost (e.g., image-pair retrieval drops no-attack ACC 0.95->0.35), and purification is effective only against clean-label images while costing ~23 hours on four A100 80GB GPUs and itself lowering clean ACC. The authors note no black-box image generation-control method exists (clean-label relies on white-box-retriever gradients) and other modalities (video/tables) are unexplored.

datasets_used: OVEN / Open-domain Visual Entity Recognition (335,135 image-text-pair corpus; UniIR-processed)
50 target queries selected per dataset; target answers generated by GPT-4 and verified distinct via LLM-as-a-Judge
quantitative_results: InfoSeek dirty-label: ASR-R 1.00, ASR-G 0.98 (Table 2); clean-label: ASR-R 0.97, ASR-G 0.94
OVEN dirty-label: ASR-R 1.00, ASR-G 0.96; clean-label: ASR-R 0.95, ASR-G 0.88
Claude-3.5 Sonnet victim: dirty-label ASR-G 0.98, clean-label ASR-G 0.94
No-attack ACC ~0.94 (InfoSeek) / ~0.81 (OVEN); drops to ~0.02-0.11 under attack
Attack budget N=5 malicious pairs per query, top-k=3, clean-label epsilon=32/255, ~400 PGD iterations (<1 min/image on one A6000)
Retriever transfer (clean/dirty ASR-R): CLIP-SF 0.97/1.00, ViT-B-32 0.90/0.99, ViT-H-14 0.80/0.98 (Table 3)
Component ablation InfoSeek (Table 6): full 0.97/0.94; Dirty w/o query-text 0.75/0.76; Base w/ query (no perturbation) 0.31/0.28
Defense-paraphrasing InfoSeek clean-label ASR-R 0.97->0.79, ASR-G 0.94->0.74; dirty-label unaffected (ASR-R stays 1.00)
Defense-duplicate-removal InfoSeek dirty-label ASR-R 1.00->0.33, ASR-G 0.98->0.54; clean-label unchanged
Defense-structure (retrieve-then-merge) dirty-label ASR-R 1.00->0.62 but no-attack ACC 0.95->0.60; image-pair retrieval drops no-attack ACC 0.95->0.35
Defense-purification (OVEN): clean-label ASR-R drops 0.65 / ASR-G drops 0.66; dirty-label ASR-R drops only 0.267 / ASR-G 0.04; cost ~23 hours on four A100 80GB GPUs
baselines_compared: CLIP PGD attack (strongest baseline: InfoSeek 0.19/0.18; OVEN 0.63/0.32)
